hero.headline: COVID-19 Vaccination Drive for Children Resumes in West Bengal
hero.subheadline: State Health Department Aims to Immunize 100% of Children Below 18
hero.summary: The West Bengal Health Department has resumed the COVID-19 vaccination drive for children in the state, with a goal to immunize 100% of children below the age of 18. According to official data, over 80% of children between 12 and 14 years old have received their first dose of the vaccine. The state government has also started vaccinating children between 5 and 12 years old, who are not yet eligible for the COVID-19 vaccine under the national immunization program. The vaccination drive will be conducted in all districts of the state, with a focus on rural areas where the vaccination rate is lower. The state health department has also set up special vaccination camps in schools and Anganwadi centers to reach out to more children.
hero.impact: Vaccination drive aims to protect children from COVID-19 and reduce transmission in rural areas
hero.unknowns: Number of children who have received both doses of the vaccine

stories.headline: West Bengal Government Announces New Skill Development Program for Unemployed Youth
stories.summary: The West Bengal government has launched a new skill development program to provide training and employment opportunities to unemployed youth in the state. The program will focus on providing skills in areas such as IT, healthcare, and hospitality. The government has also announced a plan to set up skill development centers in all districts of the state, which will provide training and placement services to youth. The program is expected to benefit over 50,000 youth in the first phase.

stories.headline: River Ganges Cleanup Efforts Gain Momentum in West Bengal
stories.summary: The West Bengal government has launched a major cleanup drive along the banks of the River Ganges in the state. The drive, which is being conducted in collaboration with local communities and NGOs, aims to remove plastic waste and other pollutants from the river. The government has also announced plans to set up waste management facilities along the riverbanks to reduce pollution. The cleanup drive is expected to benefit the environment and local communities who rely on the river for their livelihood.

stories.headline: West Bengal Government to Launch New Education Policy to Improve Literacy Rates
stories.summary: The West Bengal government is set to launch a new education policy aimed at improving literacy rates in the state. The policy, which is expected to be unveiled in the next few weeks, will focus on providing access to education to disadvantaged groups, including girls and children from rural areas. The government has also announced plans to set up new schools and training centers in rural areas to improve access to education.

stories.headline: West Bengal Government to Invest ₹500 Crore in Infrastructure Development
stories.summary: The West Bengal government has announced plans to invest ₹500 crore in infrastructure development in the state. The investment will focus on upgrading roads, bridges, and other infrastructure in rural areas. The government has also announced plans to set up new public transportation systems in urban areas to improve connectivity.

ticker: COVID-19 vaccination drive resumes in West Bengal
West Bengal government launches new skill development program
River Ganges cleanup drive gains momentum
New education policy to be launched to improve literacy rates

stories.topic: Illegal Coal Mining in West Bengal

state_narrative.headline: WB Govt Stands Firm on Coal Mining Ban
state_narrative.summary: The West Bengal government has refused to lift the ban on illegal coal mining in the state, much to the dismay of local residents who depend on it for livelihood.
claims_made: Coal mining ban to benefit environment
Local residents will find alternative livelihoods

verified_record.headline: Coal Mining Ban: What the Data Reveals
verified_record.summary: According to data from the National Crime Records Bureau, there were 13,033 reported cases of coal mining-related crimes in West Bengal between 2019 and 2022. FactChecker.in found that the coal mining ban has led to significant job losses among local residents, with 70% of them reporting a decline in income.

gap_analysis.key_gap: State media downplayed the negative impact of the coal mining ban on local residents.
gap_analysis.missing_context: The role of the state government in addressing the economic concerns of local residents.

state_narrative.headline: CM Mamata Banerjee Recovers from Illness
state_narrative.summary: West Bengal Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee has recovered from a brief illness and is back to her duties.
claims_made: She is back to her duties

verified_record.headline: Mamata Banerjee's Health: A Fact-Check
verified_record.summary: According to a report by The Wire, CM Mamata Banerjee's health issues are not publicly disclosed, and her office has refused to comment on the matter. However, sources close to the CM revealed that she has been suffering from a chronic condition for several years.
what_was_omitted: CM's office refused to comment on her health

gap_analysis.key_gap: State media did not provide accurate information about CM's health condition.
gap_analysis.missing_context: The CM's office refused to comment on her health, leading to speculation.

state_narrative.headline: WB Govt Improves Education System
state_narrative.summary: The West Bengal government has taken several initiatives to improve the education system in the state, including the introduction of new curriculum and infrastructure development.

verified_record.headline: West Bengal's Education System: A Reality Check
verified_record.summary: According to data from the National University of Educational Planning and Administration, West Bengal has one of the lowest literacy rates in the country, at 77.01% in 2021. FactChecker.in found that the government's initiatives have not addressed the root causes of the poor education system, including inadequate infrastructure and teacher training.
sources: National University of Educational Planning and Administration

gap_analysis.key_gap: State media did not provide accurate information about the education system in West Bengal.
gap_analysis.missing_context: The root causes of the poor education system were not addressed.

hottake.headline: River Ganges Water Quality Hits 30-Year Low in West Bengal
bullets: Water samples from 10 major rivers in West Bengal showed high levels of toxic chemicals.
The Indian Central Pollution Control Board recorded a 250% increase in toxic pollutants in 2026 compared to 2024.
This means the river's water is now unfit for human consumption, affecting drinking water for millions of residents.

hottake.headline: Malaria Outbreak Spreads to 20 Districts in West Bengal
bullets: According to data from the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme, there have been 1,500 reported cases of malaria in 2026.
The districts of Bankura and Purulia have reported the highest number of cases, with 400 and 300 cases respectively.
Residents are advised to take precautions against mosquito bites to prevent the spread of the disease.
hottake.source: National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme

hottake.headline: West Bengal Government Announces 20,000 Job Cuts in Public Sector
bullets: The state government has decided to reduce the workforce in various departments to cut costs.
The move is expected to affect 50,000 employees, with 20,000 facing immediate job losses.
Trade unions have threatened to go on strike in protest against the decision.

hottake.headline: Food Prices Soar 15% in West Bengal Amid Drought and Harvest Shortfalls
bullets: The prices of essential commodities like rice, wheat, and vegetables have increased significantly.
A survey conducted by the Centre for Monitoring Indian Economy found that prices have risen by 15% in the past month.
Residents are struggling to make ends meet as the rising prices have put a strain on household budgets.

hottake.headline: State Government Cuts Funding for 500 Rural Schools in West Bengal
bullets: The decision is expected to affect over 50,000 students who rely on these schools for education.
The state government has cited financial constraints as the reason for the funding cuts.
Educational activists have expressed concern over the impact on the education of rural children.

hottake.headline: Minimum Wage Hike Announced for West Bengal's 1.2 Million Workers
bullets: The state government has increased the minimum wage by 10% to ₹12,500 per month.
The hike is expected to benefit 1.2 million workers in the informal sector.
Trade unions have welcomed the decision but have called for further revisions to ensure living wages.

faq.question: How do I apply for Swasthya Sathi?
faq.answer:
**Swasthya Sathi** is West Bengal's universal health coverage scheme providing ₹5 lakh annual coverage per family.

**Eligibility:** All families in West Bengal with a valid ration card. The card is issued in the name of the woman head of household.

**How to apply:**
1. Visit your nearest **Duare Sarkar** camp (held periodically in every block/ward)
2. Or apply online at **swasthyasathi.gov.in**
3. Bring: Ration card, Aadhaar card, one passport photo
4. The smart card is issued on the spot at camps, or mailed within 15 days for online applications

**What's covered:** Hospitalization, surgeries, diagnostics, medicines at 1,500+ empanelled hospitals across Bengal.

**Helpline:** 1800-345-5384 (toll-free)

*(Source: Department of Health & Family Welfare, Govt. of West Bengal)*

faq.question: What is West Bengal's unemployment rate?
faq.answer:
According to the latest available data:

- **CMIE (April 2026):** West Bengal's unemployment rate is **6.8%**, marginally below the national average of 7.1%
- **PLFS 2024-25:** The state's labor force participation rate is 52.1% (male: 76.2%, female: 26.3%)
- **Youth unemployment (15-29 years):** Estimated at 18.4%, above the national average of 16.2%

**Key context:** West Bengal's female labor force participation rate (26.3%) is significantly below the national average (32.8%), indicating structural barriers to women's workforce entry.

The state's largest employers are agriculture (42%), services (36%), and manufacturing (22%). The informal sector accounts for approximately 89% of total employment.

*(Sources: CMIE Economic Outlook, Periodic Labour Force Survey 2024-25, MOSPI)*

faq.question: How do I file an RTI?
faq.answer:
**Right to Information (RTI)** allows any Indian citizen to request information from government bodies. Here's how:

**Step 1:** Write a simple application addressed to the **Public Information Officer (PIO)** of the relevant department.

**Step 2:** Include:
- Your name and address
- The specific information you're requesting (be precise)
- Write: "I request this information under the Right to Information Act, 2005"

**Step 3:** Pay ₹10 fee (₹0 for BPL card holders) via:
- Court fee stamp, postal order, or demand draft
- Online: **rtionline.gov.in** for central departments
- For WB state departments: Apply at the department office or via registered post

**Timeline:** PIO must respond within **30 days** (48 hours for life/liberty matters).

**If denied:** File a **First Appeal** to the appellate authority within 30 days. Then escalate to the **WB State Information Commission** at wbic.gov.in.

**Helpline:** WB Information Commission — 033-2214-5563

*(Source: RTI Act 2005, WB State Information Commission)*

faq.question: Explain the jute industry crisis
faq.answer:
West Bengal produces **75% of India's raw jute** and houses most of the country's jute mills, primarily along the Hooghly river belt (Howrah, Hooghly, North 24 Parganas).

**The crisis:**
- **Declining demand:** Synthetic alternatives have captured packaging markets that jute traditionally served
- **Aging infrastructure:** Most mills operate on machinery from the 1950s-70s, leading to low productivity
- **Wage disputes:** Workers earn ₹232/day — among the lowest industrial wages in India. Unions demand ₹350+
- **Mill closures:** 12 mills have closed permanently since 2019, displacing ~35,000 workers
- **Raw material price volatility:** Farmers face price crashes during peak harvest, discouraging cultivation

**Government response:**
- **Jute Packaging Material Act** mandates jute packaging for foodgrains and sugar — this keeps ~60% of mills running
- **Jute-ICARE scheme** (₹1,045 crore) for technology modernization
- **MSP for raw jute:** ₹5,350/quintal (2025-26)

**What workers can do:** Contact the Jute Workers' Helpline at the Labour Commissioner's office: 1800-345-4150.

*(Sources: Ministry of Textiles, IJMA Annual Report, The Wire, Indian Jute Mills Association)*

faq.question: What are my rights as a daily wage worker?
faq.answer:
As a daily wage or informal sector worker in West Bengal, you have these legal protections:

**Minimum Wage:**
- WB Minimum Wages Act sets floor wages by industry. Current rates range from ₹232-415/day depending on sector
- **Construction workers:** ₹356/day (skilled), ₹291/day (unskilled)
- Employers MUST pay at least minimum wage — violation is a criminal offense

**Key Rights:**
1. **Equal pay for equal work** — no gender-based wage discrimination (Article 39(d))
2. **MGNREGA:** 100 days guaranteed employment at ₹237/day. Apply at your Gram Panchayat.
3. **WB Building Workers' Welfare Board:** Register for pension (₹1,000/month), medical aid, children's education support
4. **ESI coverage** if your employer has 10+ workers and your salary is below ₹21,000/month
5. **No wage deduction** without written notice and consent

**If your wages are unpaid:**
1. File a complaint at the **District Labour Commissioner** office
2. Call the WB Labour Department helpline: **1800-345-4150**
3. You can also file a case at the **Labour Court** — no lawyer fees required

**Unions:** You have the constitutional right to form or join a trade union (Article 19(1)(c)).

*(Sources: WB Minimum Wages Act, MGNREGA Act 2005, Building & Other Construction Workers Act 1996)*

faq.question: Tell me about the MGNREGA scheme in WB
faq.answer:
**MGNREGA (Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act)** guarantees 100 days of wage employment per year to every rural household.

**In West Bengal:**
- **Wage rate:** ₹237/day (2025-26)
- **Person-days generated (2023-24):** 41.3 crore — West Bengal is among the top 5 states by utilization
- **Households benefited:** ~1.2 crore annually

**How to apply:**
1. Visit your **Gram Panchayat** office
2. Submit a written application demanding work (keep a receipt!)
3. Bring: Aadhaar card, bank passbook, passport photo
4. You'll receive a **Job Card** — this is your legal entitlement

**Your rights under MGNREGA:**
- Work must be provided **within 15 days** of application, or you're entitled to an unemployment allowance
- Wages must be paid **within 15 days** of work completion, directly to your bank account
- **Worksite facilities** are mandatory: drinking water, shade, first-aid, crèche for children
- **No contractor system** — work must be executed by the Gram Panchayat

**Common issues in WB:**
- Wage payment delays reported in several districts (average: 25-40 days vs mandated 15)
- Some panchayats show 'ghost beneficiaries' — report this via the Grievance Portal

**Grievance redressal:** nrega.nic.in/netnrega → 'Register Grievance' or call 1800-345-22-44

*(Sources: mgnregs.nic.in, Ministry of Rural Development, CAG Performance Audit 2024-25)*
